summary: Gruber critiques a Wall Street Journal article that frames Apple's persistent red badge prompting Apple Pay setup as heavy-handed and potentially anticompetitive. He acknowledges the badge is genuinely annoying and counterintuitive to dismiss, but faults the article for failing to mention that it can be turned off. He mocks the article's reliance on a random comedian for security expertise and finds the comparison to Microsoft's Internet Explorer bundling absurd. Gruber argues Apple Pay must be built into the OS by nature, and that by the article's logic, any new OS feature would be anticompetitive. He does concede Apple has a marketing problem explaining Apple Pay's security and convenience benefits.
key_points: The Apple Pay setup badge is genuinely annoying and counterintuitively difficult to dismiss, but the WSJ article fails to mention it can be turned off at all
The article undermines its own credibility by quoting a random comedian over payment security experts on Apple Pay's security
Comparing Apple Pay's setup badge to Microsoft's IE bundling is absurd because Apple Pay, unlike IE, is fundamentally an OS-level feature that cannot exist separately
Apple has a real marketing problem: many people don't understand Apple Pay's security advantages and convenience, particularly Apple Pay Cash
With 20 years of hindsight, browsers did belong built into operating systems — the real competition was about quality, not bundling
money_quotes: It is inscrutably counterintuitive to need to tap a button that says 'Set Up Apple Pay' when your intention is to stop being nagged to set it up because you don't want to set up Apple Pay.
That's just wrong, and renders the remainder of his article moot.

Experts say using Apple Pay is faster and more secure than using a credit card, but here's a 29-year-old comedian who thinks otherwise. Who wouldn't take credit card security advice from a completely random guy on the street?
Netscape didn't lose to IE because IE was built into Windows; it lost because IE was better.
Apple Pay, as conceived, has to be built into the OS. By Kay's argument, most of the built-in features in any OS are anticompetitive.
What new features could be added to iOS without hurting some sort of competitor?
Probably because they were stifling laughter.
key_insight: The WSJ article starts with a legitimate UX complaint about Apple Pay's nagging badge but undermines itself by omitting the fix, citing unqualified sources, and making a ludicrous antitrust comparison to Microsoft's IE bundling.
